answers.intake_q1: When did your elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly on your elbow the pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: Any recent injury or repetitive activity involving your elbow?
answers.intake_a3: The pain started while I was playing tennis
answers.intake_q4: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow?
answers.intake_q5: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your arm or hand?
answers.intake_q6: Any difficulty moving your elbow or pain when you try to grip or lift objects?
answers.intake_q7: Have you tried any treatments or medications for your elbow pain, and if so, what was the effect?
answers.intake_a7: I iced it the first day and it helped a little with the pain
answers.intake_q8: Any fever, chills, or other systemic symptoms since the elbow pain started?
answers.intake_q9: Any prior history of elbow problems or arthritis?
answers.intake_summary: A patient presents with 3 weeks of lateral elbow pain that started while playing tennis, with pain on lifting objects but no swelling, numbness, or systemic symptoms. There is no prior elbow history. Initial icing provided slight relief.

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of pain localized to the outside of her elbow. The pain began while playing tennis and is aggravated by lifting objects. She reports no swelling, redness, warmth, numbness, tingling, weakness, or systemic symptoms such as fever or chills. Initial self-treatment with ice provided slight relief. There is no prior history of elbow problems or arthritis. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical examination or vital signs available.

A: 
The clinical presentation is consistent with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), characterized by lateral elbow pain exacerbated by gripping or lifting activities and absence of systemic or neurological symptoms. 

Differential: 
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) 
2. Radial tunnel syndrome 
3. Osteoarthritis of the elbow 
4. Referred pain from cervical radiculopathy 

P: 
- First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen for pain relief and inflammation reduction. 
- Alternatives: Acetaminophen if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
- Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with gastrointestinal, renal, or cardiovascular risk factors; avoid in patients with known allergies to NSAIDs. Acetaminophen should be used within recommended dosing limits to avoid hepatotoxicity. 
- Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek medical attention if pain worsens, if there is development of swelling, redness, warmth, numbness, weakness, or if systemic symptoms such as fever occur. 
- Testing: Not indicated at this time based on clinical presentation; consider imaging or referral if symptoms persist beyond 6-8 weeks or if diagnosis is unclear.

evidence.title: Tennis elbow - NHS
evidence.snippet: rvices without needing a referral from a GP. Get NHS help for back or joint problems Treatments for tennis elbow Tennis elbow usually gets better after resting your arm for a few weeks, but it can sometimes last longer. A GP may give you anti-inflammatory tablets. They may also refer you for physiotherapy if your symptoms have not improved after trying treat…

evidence.title: content.txt
evidence.snippet: Check if you have tennis elbow The main symptom of tennis elbow is pain on the outside of your elbow. The pain may range from mild discomfort when you move your arm to constant pain that can affect your sleep. It may be worse when: - lifting or bending your arm - gripping objects - moving your wrist Other symptoms of tennis elbow include: - tenderness or swe…

evidence.title: Elbow Injuries | Elbow Disorders | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: bone, cartilage, ligaments and fluid. Muscles and tendons help the elbow joint move. When any of these structures is hurt or diseased, you have elbow problems. Many things can make your elbow hurt. A common cause is tendinitis , an inflammation or injury to the tendons that attach muscle to bone. Tendinitis of the elbow is a sports injury , often from playin…

evidence.title: Tendinitis: MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: onnect muscles to bones. They help your muscles move your bones. Tendinitis is the severe swelling of a tendon. Tendinitis usually happens after repeated injury to an area such as the wrist or ankle. It causes pain and soreness around a joint. Some common forms of tendinitis are named after the sports that increase their risk. They include tennis elbow, golf…
